perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information : means all confidential information (however recorded, preserve d or disclosed) disclosed by a P arty or its Representatives to the other Party and that Party's Representatives including but not limited to: (a) the fact that discussions a nd negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; (b) the existence and terms of this Agreement; (c) any information relating to: (i) the business, affairs, custo mers, clients, suppliers, plans , int entions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party or of the Disclosing Party's Affiliates ; and (ii) the operations, processes, product information, know -how, designs, specifications, trade secrets , computer programs or software of the Disclosing P arty or of the Disclosing Party's Affiliates ; and (d) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information : means all confidential information disclosed by a Party to the other Party including but not limited to: (a) the fact that discussions a nd negotiations are taking place concerning the Purpose and the status of those discussions and negotiations; (b) the existence and terms of this Agreement; (c) any information relating to: (i) the business, affairs, plans , int entions, or market opportunities of the Disclosing Party; and (d) any information or analysis derived from Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'however recorded, preserved or disclosed', 'customers, clients, suppliers', and 'Affiliates', as well as the complete subsection (ii), the definition of 'Confidential Information' is significantly narrowed. This introduces an in-text contradiction because later clauses refer to broader categories of information as 'Confidential Information'. For example, discussions with suppliers may no longer be protected, despite clauses assuming their confidentiality. The in-text contradiction arises when enforcing the confidentiality provisions, as it's unclear which information is actually covered.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In consideration of the mutual disclosure of Confidential Information the Recipient Party undertakes: - (a) to keep secret and confidential all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party; (b) to use or apply the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party solely for the Purpose and so as to determine whether or not and on what terms the Parties might wish to proceed; (c) not to use, copy, adapt, alter, disclose or part with possession of or apply the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for any other purpose or its own purposes other than as described in paragraph (b) above;
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' by removing 'however recorded, preserved or disclosed', 'customers, clients, suppliers', and 'Affiliates', as well as subsection (ii). This directly contradicts the contradicted text, which broadly obligates the Recipient Party to keep secret 'all Confidential Information'. The narrowed definition means certain information, like supplier discussions, might no longer be considered confidential, creating a direct conflict with the confidentiality obligations. This fits the 'Omissions – In Text Contradiction' type as essential language was removed, creating a contradiction with obligations stated in other sections.

perturbation.original_text: 6. Term and Termination 6.1 If either Party decides not to become, or continue to be involved in the Purpose it shall notify the other Party in writing immediately. The obligations of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or di scussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of five (5) years from the termination of this A greement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. Term and Termination 6.1 If either Party decides not to become, or continue to be involved in the Purpose it shall notify the other Party in writing immediately. The obligations of each Party shall continue after the termination of this A greement.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the phrase 'notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or di scussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of five (5) years from the termination of this A greement' and replacing it with 'shall continue after the termination of this A greement', the duration of confidentiality obligations becomes indefinite. This creates uncertainty and an in-text contradiction with the implied reasonable duration of such agreements under general legal principles. The lack of a defined term could be challenged in court.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. Term and Termination 6.1 If either Party decides not to become, or continue to be involved in the Purpose it shall notify the other Party in writing immediately. The obligations of each Party shall continue after the termination of this A greement. 6.2 Termination of this Agreement shall not affect any accrued rights or remedies to whic h either Party is entitled.
perturbation.justification: The original text specified a five-year period for obligations after termination. The changed text removes this five-year limit, making the obligations indefinite. This creates a direct conflict because the contradicted text implicitly assumes a reasonable duration for the obligations, which is now undermined by the lack of a defined term. The original text and changed text are located in the same area, but the change in the new text now contradicts what would be considered legally binding in the contradicted text by making the time frame in the contradicted text undefined.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Disclosure to Third P arties The Recipient Party shall have the rig ht to disclose the Confidential Information to a third party involved in the Purpose, subject to the Recipient Party obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party to such disclosure and the Recipient Party agrees that it shall procure that th e third party shall first enter into a written acknowledgement and undertaking of confidentiality in favour of both Parties to this Agreement and in a form which is acceptable to the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Disclosure to Third P arties The Recipient Party shall have the rig ht to disclose the Confidential Information to a third party involved in the Purpose, subject to the Recipient Party obtaining the prior written consent of the Disclosing Party to such disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: By removing 'and the Recipient Party agrees that it shall procure that th e third party shall first enter into a written acknowledgement and undertaking of confidentiality in favour of both Parties to this Agreement and in a form which is acceptable to the Disclosing Party', the requirement for the third party to sign a confidentiality agreement is eliminated. This creates an in-text contradiction because the Disclosing Party might reasonably assume that any third party receiving confidential information is bound by a confidentiality agreement. It creates ambiguity and the potential for breaches of confidentiality without recourse. The in-text contradiction arises as the overall intent of the agreement is to protect information, but this provision weakens that protection substantially.
perturbation.contradicted_text: In consideration of the mutual disclosure of Confidential Information the Recipient Party undertakes: - (a) to keep secret and confidential all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party; (b) to use or apply the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party solely for the Purpose and so as to determine whether or not and on what terms the Parties might wish to proceed; (c) not to use, copy, adapt, alter, disclose or part with possession of or apply the Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party for any other purpose or its own purposes other than as described in paragraph (b) above;
perturbation.justification: The original text required the Recipient Party to ensure any third party receiving confidential information signed a confidentiality agreement. The changed text removes this requirement. The contradicted text states that the Recipient Party undertakes to keep secret and confidential all Confidential Information of the Disclosing Party. By removing the requirement for third parties to sign a confidentiality agreement, the changed text directly contradicts the Recipient Party's undertaking to keep all confidential information secret, as third parties are no longer explicitly bound by confidentiality, which creates a strong in-text contradiction according to the Omissions - In Text Contradiction definition.
